proofs.proof_tex:
Write \(S(x,y)=(y,x)\), and extend \(S\) coordinatewise to \(n\) observations. The mirror construction gives, pointwise,
\[p_{-,\delta}(x,y)=p_{+,\delta}(S(x,y)),\qquad \ell_\delta(S(x,y))=-\ell_\delta(x,y).	ag{1}\]
All densities are positive because \(b=1-\alpha^2>0\). Consequently the likelihood-ratio rule is Borel. Away from its tie set it is antisymmetric under \(S\), and on the tie set def:mirror-equalizer-rule makes it antisymmetric except on \(F_n=\{x_i=y_i	ext{ for all }i\}\). The set \(F_n\) has Lebesgue, hence \(P_{h,\delta}^n\)-, measure zero. A change of variables by the measure-preserving involution \(S\) therefore gives
\[P_{+,\delta}^n\{\pi_{n,\delta}^{LR}=-\}=P_{-,\delta}^n\{\pi_{n,\delta}^{LR}=+\}.	ag{2}\]
Let
\[B_n(\delta)= rac12\inf_{\pi\in T_n}\left[P_{+,\delta}^n\{\pi=-\}+P_{-,\delta}^n\{\pi=+\}ight]= rac12\int\min(p_{+,\delta}^n,p_{-,\delta}^n).	ag{3}\]
The likelihood-ratio rule attains the pointwise minimum in (3), including on ties. By (2), each of its two errors equals their average \(B_n(\delta)\). Conversely, the maximum error of any rule is at least its average error, which is at least \(B_n(\delta)\). Thus, for every allowed \(n,\delta\),
\[R_n(\delta)=B_n(\delta)=P_{+,\delta}^n\{\pi_{n,\delta}^{LR}
e+\}=P_{-,\delta}^n\{\pi_{n,\delta}^{LR}
e-\}.	ag{4}\]
This also covers \(\delta=0\): the two laws coincide and antisymmetric tie breaking splits the common law equally, up to the null fixed-point set.

We next obtain the local tilted moments needed for both regimes. Relative to \(P_0\), the likelihood identities established in thm:local-geometry are
\[L_{+,\delta}= rac{p_{+,\delta}}{p_0}=\exp\left\{\delta v_+- rac{\delta^2g(X)^2}{2b}ight\},\qquad L_{-,\delta}= rac{p_{-,\delta}}{p_0}=\exp\left\{\delta v_-- rac{\delta^2g(Y)^2}{2b}ight\}.	ag{5}\]
Put
\[d=v_+-v_-,\qquad q= rac{g(Y)^2-g(X)^2}{2b}.	ag{6}\]
Then the one-observation log likelihood ratio has the exact expansion
\[\ell_\delta=\delta d+\delta^2q.	ag{7}\]
Let \(A_\delta=\int\sqrt{p_{+,\delta}p_{-,\delta}}\) and \(M_\delta=A_\delta^{-1}\sqrt{L_{+,\delta}L_{-,\delta}}\), so \(M_\delta=d m_\delta/dP_0\). Mirror symmetry makes \(m_\delta\) invariant under \(S\), while (1) makes \(\ell_\delta\) antisymmetric. Hence
\[E_{m_\delta}\ell_\delta=0.	ag{8}\]
We justify all moment passages explicitly. Under \(P_0\), set \(a=Y-\alpha X\) and \(c=X-\alpha Y\). Completing squares in (5) yields, for every \(\delta\),
\[\sqrt{L_{+,\delta}L_{-,\delta}}=\exp\left\{ rac{\delta a g(X)+\delta c g(Y)}{2b}- rac{\delta^2(g(X)^2+g(Y)^2)}{4b}ight\}\le \exp\left\{ rac{a^2+c^2}{4b}ight\}=:H.	ag{9}\]
The Gaussian vector \((a,c)\) has covariance \(b egin{pmatrix}1&-\alpha\-\alpha&1\end{pmatrix}\). Therefore \(E_{P_0}H^r<\infty\) for every \(1<r<2/(1+\alpha)\); this interval is nonempty by ass:linear-center. Ass:exponential-envelope gives every polynomial moment of \(g(X)\) and \(g(Y)\), and Gaussianity gives every polynomial moment of \(a,c\). Holder's inequality applied with such an \(r>1\) consequently shows, for some \(\kappa\in(0,1]\),
\[E_{P_0}\left[H\{ |d|+\delta_0|q|\}^{2+\kappa}ight]<\infty.	ag{10}\]
Thm:local-geometry gives \(A_\delta=e^{-C_\star(\delta)}	o1\). Equations (7), (9), and (10), followed by dominated convergence, now give the stronger form of the asserted tilted second-moment bound,
\[E_{m_\delta}\ell_\delta^2=\delta^2E_{P_0}\left[M_\delta(d+\delta q)^2ight]=\delta^2\{E_{P_0}d^2+o(1)\}=c_2\delta^2+o(\delta^2),	ag{11}\]
and also the uniform Lyapunov bound
\[\sup_{0<|\delta|<\delta_1}E_{m_\delta}|\ell_\delta/\delta|^{2+\kappa}<\infty	ag{12}\]
for some \(\delta_1>0\). In particular, along every \(\delta_n\downarrow0\), \(E_{m_n}\ell_{\delta_n}^2=O(\delta_n^2)\), as claimed. Notice that (9)--(10) also establish the integrability needed for (8); no moment or conditioning step is implicit.

We now express the exact minimax risk under the tilted law. Symmetry of the Chernoff integral about \(t=1/2\), proved in thm:local-geometry, gives \(A_\delta=e^{-C_\star(\delta)}\). For \(L_n=\sum_{i=1}^n\ell_{\delta_n}(X_i,Y_i)\), the identities \(p_{+,\delta_n}=A_nm_ne^{\ell_{\delta_n}/2}\) and \(p_{-,\delta_n}=A_nm_ne^{-\ell_{\delta_n}/2}\), combined with (3)--(4), yield
\[R_n(\delta_n)= rac{A_n^n}{2}E_{m_n^n}e^{-|L_n|/2}= rac{e^{-nC_\star(\delta_n)}}2E_{m_n^n}e^{-|L_n|/2}.	ag{13}\]
This formula incorporates likelihood ties and is the key usable equalizer identity.

Suppose first that \(nC_m(\delta_n)	oho\in[0,\infty)\). By thm:local-geometry,
\[n\delta_n^2\longrightarrow\lambda:=ho/c_1,\qquad nC_\star(\delta_n)\longrightarrow	au^2/8,\qquad 	au^2:=c_2\lambda=ho c_2/c_1.	ag{14}\]
If \(ho>0\), (8), (11), and (12) imply \(L_n\Rightarrow N(0,	au^2)\) under \(m_n^n\). For completeness, this triangular-array central limit step follows directly from characteristic functions: for fixed \(t\), the inequality \(|e^{iu}-1-iu+u^2/2|\le K_\kappa|u|^{2+\kappa}\) and (8), (12) give
\[E_{m_n}e^{it\ell_{\delta_n}}=1- rac{t^2}{2}E_{m_n}\ell_{\delta_n}^2+O(\delta_n^{2+\kappa}).	ag{15}\]
Here \(n\delta_n^{2+\kappa}	o0\), while (11) and (14) give \(nE_{m_n}\ell_{\delta_n}^2	o	au^2\); taking the \(n\)-th power in (15) proves the displayed Gaussian convergence. Since \(z\mapsto e^{-|z|/2}\) is bounded and continuous, (13)--(14) imply, for \(G\sim N(0,	au^2)\),
\[R_n(\delta_n)\longrightarrow rac{e^{-	au^2/8}}2E e^{-|G|/2}=e^{-	au^2/8}E[e^{G/2}1\{G<0\}]=\Phi(-	au/2).	ag{16}\]
The last equality is obtained by completing the square in the normal density. If \(ho=0\), (11) gives \(E_{m_n^n}L_n^2=nE_{m_n}\ell_{\delta_n}^2	o0\), so \(L_n	o0\) in probability. Also \(nC_\star(\delta_n)	o0\). Bounded convergence in probability in (13) gives \(R_n(\delta_n)	o1/2=\Phi(0)\). Together these cases prove
\[R_n(\delta_n)\longrightarrow\Phi\left(- rac12\sqrt{ho c_2/c_1}ight).	ag{17}\]

Finally suppose \(nC_m(\delta_n)	o\infty\). Thm:local-geometry implies \(N_n:=nC_\star(\delta_n)	o\infty\), \(N_n\asymp n\delta_n^2\), and \(C_\star(\delta_n)/C_m(\delta_n)	o c_2/(8c_1)\). By (8) and (11),
\[\operatorname{Var}_{m_n^n}(L_n)=nE_{m_n}\ell_{\delta_n}^2=O(N_n).	ag{18}\]
Take \(a_n=N_n^{3/4}\). Chebyshev's inequality gives \(m_n^n\{|L_n|\le a_n\}=1-o(1)\). Hence (13) yields the two-sided bound
\[ rac{e^{-N_n}}2e^{-a_n/2}\{1-o(1)\}\le R_n(\delta_n)\le rac{e^{-N_n}}2.	ag{19}\]
Because \(a_n=o(N_n)\), taking negative logarithms proves
\[ rac{-\log R_n(\delta_n)}{nC_\star(\delta_n)}\longrightarrow1.	ag{20}\]
Multiplying (20) by \(C_\star(\delta_n)/C_m(\delta_n)	o c_2/(8c_1)\) gives
\[ rac{-\log R_n(\delta_n)}{nC_m(\delta_n)}\longrightarrow rac{c_2}{8c_1}.	ag{21}\]
All constants here belong to the one fixed mirror path. The proof uses no lower envelope over a global ANM class, so both moving-alternative conclusions are pointwise exactly as stated.
